front: Symphony: two sets of timpani duel in the finale — placed on opposite sides, playing tritones — before a 'glorioso' coda; Nielsen wrote it depicts the 'elemental will to live'
work: The Timpani Duel in Nielsen's 'Inextinguishable' Symphony

front: Concerto: opens with four soft D's on solo timpani before the rest of the orchestra enters
Symphony: nicknamed 'Drumroll' for its opening timpani roll; lower strings then play a slow 3/4 Dies Irae melody
Mass: nicknamed 'Paukenmesse' for the ominous timpani solo in the Agnus Dei, which follows a foreboding Benedictus
work: Haydn's Mass in Time of War – Paukenmesse

front: Symphony: earliest major work with mid-performance timpani retuning; timpanist retuned G-drum up to A in the first movement; German nickname 'Paukenschlag' from the fortissimo second-movement chord
Timpani: large copper-bodied drums tuned by foot pedal (Dresden pedal system, late 1800s); developed from the Arabic naker; only percussion in Beethoven's first eight symphonies
Timpani: Grieg's Piano Concerto opens with a crescendo roll before the soloist enters; Also sprach Zarathustra responds to the trumpet fanfare with triplets on C and G
Timpani: Berlioz's Requiem calls for 16 of them; Sibelius's Symphony No. 1 opens under a clarinet solo and uses them to introduce the scherzo theme
Timpani: Prokofiev's Romeo and Juliet uses 15 straight notes to signify Tybalt's death; Elliott Carter wrote Eight Pieces for Four Timpani; Philip Glass wrote a Concerto Fantasy for two timpanists
